speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP] [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Barbara Gordon, and I'm continuing the interview with Erika Jacoby in Valley Village. And it's today, July the 11th, 1994. Erika, tell us about your leaving the ghetto, and how your life was formed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were many rumors about the-- what was going to happen to us. And my, my mother and I decided that we should hide some of our valuables. And my mother hid some of our-- some of her jewelry in, in the basement under the coal-- she dug. And we also carried down into the basement my father's set of the Talmud, which we were able to recover after the war. We also hid some, some of the stuff that we thought were very important in our lives-- such as soap-- in a certain a place in the bathroom, which we could later recover. News came out every day, what we can do and what we cannot do. And then one day, it was announced that we will be taken somewhere else. And they called us to line up outside the court yard, the whole building. And then they Hungarian gendarmes came in. And they searched us. They searched our bodies. The-- they actually searched our bodies manually to see if we were hiding anything. And then they told us to line up outside the gate of our building, and take with us whatever we could carry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know that because I was 15, and I was so, so-- such a idealist, I did not think of anything that could save my life. But I brought with me things that were important in my life. I brought with me pictures, and my diary, and I had a notebook where I put down important quotations from my readings that I accumulated throughout my life. And then in the last minute, my mother said, well where is your sweater? And I stuck in a sweater there, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother, she told me later she did put on, on-- into this bag all kinds of provisions, and that other people as well brought a lot of food with them. Now, the people that stayed with us, they were able to hire a horse and buggy. And they put some of the stuff on there. There were-- there were only a few of these people who could afford that. Most of the people had to schlep their stuff on their backs. And we walked. Maybe we walked about 10 miles until we arrived to the outskirts of the city, which was called the brick factory. They probably manufactured bricks there, but we were not taken into any manufacturing place, but where the bricks dried, which were these covered shelters without any walls. And we were given straw on the floor, on the cement floor, and each group assigned to a different shelt-- different covered area. And we were told that that would be our place then. There were no bathrooms. So for-- I remember some of the people-- well, there were no men with us. These were mostly older people and mothers with young children. And so I remember that we, we were asked to come and dig.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that's going to be really loud. I wonder if we can pick it up--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, pick it up from--

speaker: interviewee
text: The truck?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, the truck. The truck's very loud.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the-- that they were all, the older men--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- there were only older men and mostly young mothers and children who were with us. So we would ask the young girls to go and help dig some trenches that we could use as latrines. But the trenches were not hidden, so that it was in full sight of everybody if you had to go to the bathroom. And I remember for three days I would not go, but eventually, I did go, and there were separate trenches for the men and the women. Now, this was a very confusing time because there were no-- there was no order. There was a community kitchen where women went and, and, and cooked. And I remember eating the first time in my life fried onions on bread and how wonderful it tasted. And I was surprised that we never had that before. Now, I organized a group of kids who decided-- of my age-- that we decided to help the old people. And especially one of the shelters was assigned for the people who were brought out from the hospital. These were old people who were-- who either had serious illnesses or were unable to take care of themselves. And I remember how we tried to feed them, and they would spit up or they would vomit. And then we learned how to clean them up because they were no longer able to use the bathroom. And I remember how, how much it took me to overcome my repulsion and do this. But I also think that it helped me feel that I had a purpose, that I had done something good, and wasn't just there for nothing. One day, my grandparents, and my aunts, and uncles, and cousins were arriving from [? Italy. ?] Each day a new transport came in, and so pretty soon, we were thousands of people there. And the new transport arrived on top of a hill with a train, which was outside of this area. And we went up there, and there was a fence. I saw my grandparents being shoved out of the-- of the wagon. And I, I jumped over this fence, and I carried my grandfather down this hill on my back and took him to, to the place where they were assigned to.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather had suffered, previously, several strokes, and he was very weak. And he was not quite conscious. He was in and out of consciousness. And then when we got him down there, he asked me, where are we? And I could not explain to him where we were [BACKGROUND NOISE] because he could not understand it. And so finally, I said to him, you know, this is the times before the coming of the Messiah, and you know that, that before the coming of the Messiah, people go through a lot of difficulties and suffering. And he somehow calmed down with that and was able to accept it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were suffering a lot, these old people who had no one to take care of them. And mostly I think the fact that we didn't know what was going to happen to us. We paid the Hungarians gendarmes some money, and they gave us some news about what was coming. And they said that we would be taken to a camp where we would be fed and we would work, and the-- and the older people will be taking care of the younger children. Now one day-- it was about 10 or 11 days after we had left there-- we were told to get our belongings together and go up on the hill. And we were put into the wagons. We were squeezed in about 100 people into the wagon. I was not with my grandparents, but I was with my mother in the same wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these train car wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Train car wagons, right. Cattle cars. And it was so crowded that we could not practically breathe. I know from other people that there were some pails there for using as a bathroom, or another one for drinking. I don't remember seeing them. All I remember, feeling suffocated and how my mother tried to push me to the window so I could get some air. And of course, the little children actually suffocated, who could not be held up. And there was an, an old man who was next to me, and he kind of collapsed. And I recognized him as my neighbor, who I never knew was Jewish, who used to chase us away from front of his house. But looks like one of his ancestors was Jewish, so he ended up with the same fate as we did. We traveled for three days. We didn't know where we were going. And when the train finally stopped, I saw the sign, Auschwitz-Birkenau. I never saw the-- I never heard about the place before. And we were-- the, the doors were unlocked, and we were yelled at by now the German officers. Remember, in Hungary, we were mostly taken care of by the Hungarian gendarmes. But here were the Nazi officers who, with their whips in their hands, with their dogs, they yelled at us, [GERMAN]. And then there were some prisoners in prisoner garbs who then helped us getting out. We, we were told to line up one after the other, and shoved into a line. And I held on to my mother, but then my cousin, my aunt asked her to hold the baby. And so my mother held the baby, and I left my mother. And I went with the other younger people. And I passed by this very attractive man-- this officer, who told me, go ahead, in German. And I passed by, and I was taken to the place where the working people were taken. I was halfway there when suddenly I see my mother behind me. And I looked back, and I saw my grandparents. And I saw my grandparents talking and yelling to my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I waved to them. And I said, I'll see you tonight. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My mother ran after me. She said she went in front of Mengele and said to him, I can work. I'm strong. So she caught up with me, and I said, what happened? She said my grandmother told her not to leave me alone because there were so many German soldiers around, and it wasn't safe. So my grandm-- my mother gave the baby back to the mother, and thus she saved her life, which she really didn't know at that time. She felt bad that she had to give the baby back, but that was her mother's wish. So she caught up with me, and we were marching. And we were marching I don't know how long.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were taken to a big hall. A big hall, that's all I remember. And we were told to get undressed. And this is the first time in my life that I had to get undressed in front of men and take everything off. And I could not believe it that that's what I had to do. And then I saw everybody doing it, so I knew that that's what I had to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we, we stayed there all night, naked. And it was-- I remember-- as I remember, now, seeing one light bulb, one light bulb, and it was dark when the light bulb went out. And we-- as, as, as it became dark, people started to yell and scream. And they started to cry. Where is my mother? Where is my child? And so on. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then next morning, we were taken to, to a bath. Now, since I didn't know that there was another kind of bath as well, we were kind of happy that we were taken to a bath. We were then shaven. Our hair was shaven, our body hair was shaven. And eventually given a piece of dress, a dress-- no underwear or shoes or anything. And we put on this dress. These were dresses that were taken away probably from those people that arrived, and they didn't fit. And my mother looked ridiculous in a transparent evening gown. And it was long, and I-- and, and we had no hair. And we looked at each other, and I think we started to laugh because we-- it-- we looked so pathetic. And I could not stand that my mother's hair was-- head was uncovered. My mother was a religious woman. Her hair was always covered.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tore off a piece of her dress from the bottom of the dress. But unfortunately, the dress was cut on a bias, and it made a slit high up on her leg. So now she was exposed on her leg, but we made something for her head so she could cover her head.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken to a camp. And this was not like the other camps. This was like unfinished. And we were taken to a very big room, and, and slept on the floor-- on a cement floor. And this is the first time that we were given food. Now, the food came in a big pot, and we had to-- there were no spoons or, or bowls to eat from. We were told that everybody has to drink from that. And so we passed it around. And when I first tasted it, I, I could not stand it. And the next time it came around to me, I, I drank it because I know if-- I knew if I didn't, I wouldn't survive. And as a matter of fact, later on, we, we, we counted how each of us swallowed-- how many s-- s-- swallowed so we make sure that we didn't drink more than our allotted share. My friend, who was my best friend in Hungary, she refused to drink it. And I tried to beg her and, and yelled at her to drink it. And she says, no, I'm not going to drink it. And I said, you won't survive. She says, I don't care if I don't survive. And indeed, she didn't survive. And for years, I dreamt with her running and racing together, and she falling behind. And I had such a feeling of, of guilt that I allowed her to give up. Now in this place, we stayed only 10 days. And after 10 days, we were selected to work, which was a wonderful thing. And we were taken into the wagons again, into the cattle cars, and taken to a place called Auschwitz-- Plaszow. And Plaszow was near Krakow, and it was a family camp, where families were-- like a ghetto, and families lived together before we arrived. Upon our arrival, they either took away or killed all the young children, so that they made space for us. The place was much more homey than Auschwitz. We had barracks, and we had bunk beds where we slept. But the work was unbearable. We had to hew stones out of mines. We had to build roads. We had to take apart a Jewish cemetery and use it to build the roads with it. And we had to carry heavy loads of, of German uniforms over ravines over a plank, and we were told to run. And if we couldn't hold our balance, we would fall, fall in. We were also suffering from malnutrition, and we had boils all over our bodies, and had bed bugs that would then sit on these boils. And by this time, we lost so much weight that our, our cheeks were sunken in. And sometimes we had bed bugs making nests in our cheeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were here for about, about two months. And the Russian army was getting closer and closer. And there were cannons and shootings almost every night. And there were also [INAUDIBLE] often for the purpose of witnessing hangings of those that tried to get away. There was one boy that I met one day when I was rummaging through the garbage and looking for a pot. And that reminds me, it may have been a one of Schindler's pots. And he-- and because this was where Schindler's movie took place. And I, I was whipped by an officer, a German officer, because I was talking to this young man. And I started to cry. And he told me, don't you ever cry. Don't you ever show them that it hurts. And this was one of the young men that were later caught escaping and hung. And I couldn't not cry. [SIGHS] We were taken again back to Auschwitz after two months because of the advancing Russian army. And in Auschwitz, this time we knew what was going to happen to us. And we knew there was going to be a selection. And we knew what b-- bad condition we were in. So my mother was the weakest. She's developed some kind of a fever every day, and she was very ill. And I saw how Mengele then made the selections, and most of my cousins already were selected to go to the gas chamber. And I saw that anybody had any kind of a paper bandage, which we used to cover our sores, would automatically go to the right side. And I quickly tore off the bandage off my mother's leg, and I gave her a shove into her back. And she straightened out and she passed. And then I passed too, and my aunt who was always with us as well. We passed. And then we were taken again to the, to the baths. And we were shaven again. Our hair was shaven even though we had already about an inch of hair. And then we were given our number tattooed on our-- on our arm, and-- which was kind of a hopeful sign that if we were given a tattoo, maybe we will be, again, taken somewhere else. We stayed six weeks in Auschwitz. We had many, many interesting events that happened to us in Auschwitz. I just want to tell you one that was very important. And I don't think I have time to describe Auschwitz. And I'm sure that it's well known. But we were-- one, one day, we were taken to a bath. We were taken to a bath about once a week or once every other week. And as we were marching, we were go-- we were going by a German officer's area. And I saw a swimming pool. And I jumped out of the line, and I dove into the swimming pool. And I swam across the pool, and I got back. And they didn't shoot me, and I survived. But of course, my mother almost died. I mean, it was such an irr-- irresponsible act from my part. But I was young, and I, I needed to be alive. And I did that. Another important event-- we went another time also to the bathes, and we saw my uncle working with the bricks there. He knew that we were alive, and he was hoping that he would one day see us. And he prepared-- on our way back, he prepared for us some bread and a sweater for me. And, and he ran out, and he gave us a kiss and a hug. And unfortunately, he didn't survive. He was-- he stayed in the Auschwitz until they, they eventually-- they exploded the crematorium, and that's where he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive other clothing that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had a dress. It was a gray dress with-- no longer, we didn't-- we had-- because we had on-- our numbers on our arm, we no longer had our numbers on the dress. And we received a wooden platform canvas shoe that we then-- which lasted throughout our-- there were everyday bombings and air raids in Auschwitz-- not at Auschwitz, but around Auschwitz, the factories there. And I remember I used to say, well, this is a good sign. Again, this is because it was the month of the Jewish Elul, and that this is when we used to blow the shofar every day. And that was the sign that there will be a redemption. Somehow I always talked myself into some kind of a hope. There was an incident that we walked one day and I found a little knife-- maybe a child's toy knife-- and a-- and a necklace with a Magen David on it, a gold necklace. And I was so excited and happy on it. And because my dress was somewhat more open, I put it on my mother's neck to wear it. And one day, the lageralteste, or the blockalteste, saw it. She was a Jewish woman. She was very, very cruel. And she yanked it off her neck. And my mother became inflamed, and she took the courage to yell at this woman. And she said shame on you, you take away one little thing that my daughter has. Now for that you must reward her. My mother demanded that they must reward me with something. And so I got an extra ladle of, of, of food from this blockalteste who somehow was stunned by mother's-- by my mother's courage. My mother told her, shame on you, you Jewish girl, how you treat a little child like that. My, my aunt, who was kind of a [INAUDIBLE]. She was young, a few years older. She went to the-- to the latrines. The latrines were the place where we usually exchanged news, and that was just one of the barracks with about 100 holes on a-- on a platform. And the holes were large enough so that my aunt one night-- because she had dysentery-- she fell into the latrine. And we couldn't-- we, we pulled her out, and I don't know exactly how she got cleaned up. But she had to line up for [GERMAN], and we wrapped her in some cement bags. And she was punished, I don't know how severely, for being in a ce-- in a cement bag. Now, after Auschwitz, we were taken to another working camp. And this was near Bunzlau. The name of the camp was Wiesau. This was the best camp we were in. The, the lagerkommander was a very gentle, fatherly kind of a person. We arrived on Erev Rosh Hashana-- on the eve of Rosh Hashana. And he made a speech to us, wishing us a happy New Year. And he gave us off the following day because it was Rosh Hashana. It was a more homey type of an atmosphere. But the work was, again, very backbreaking. And we, we did construction. We constructed railroads, and we laid down pipes. My mother was still very ill. Now, I did not mention that in, in Auschwitz, where we were about 10 of us on a-- on a platform where we sat, I used to cover her with my body because she used to shiver at night from the fever that she used to have.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at this camp, she was still very ill. And I-- one day, I got an onion from one of the Wehrmacht soldiers who supervised us at work. And I snuck into the boiler room, and I toasted my bread for her. And I smeared it with this onion so that she would eat a little bit because she had no appetite at all-- lost all her appetite. She eventually got better, but she was able to work in the potato area where they peeled potatoes for the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember once I was able to get into the garbage. And I, I got a lot of goodies out of there. And I almost got killed and suffocated because a truck brought more garbage on and poured it over me, and I was almost suffocated there. After, I don't know, about two or three months when the ground froze and we could no longer work there, we were, again, loaded into wagons and taken to another camp.
